coredata.dc:title: Impact of ternary nanocomposite In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs composition on low ppm NO2 sensing performance

coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen dioxide (NO2) is a significant air pollutant with a notable environmental impact, necessitating precise monitoring. However, most existing NO2 sensing materials exhibit low sensitivity and require high-temperature operation. In this study, we employed a hydrothermal and co-precipitation method to synthesize a novel ternary nanocomposite, In2O3/SnO2 nanoparticles (NPs)/fluorine-doped carbon quantum dots (F-CDs). By varying the mass ratio of In2O3 to SnO2 NPs, we investigated the gas-sensing performance of the composite towards NO2. The results demonstrate that when the ratio of SnO2 and In2O3 reaches to 1 : 2, the 2-In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs gas sensor exhibited the highest response, excellent selectivity, and remarkable stability under ultraviolet (UV) light irradiation at room temperature (∼25 °C). Furthermore, the response of the 2-In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs gas sensor displayed a strong linear relationship with the concentration of NO2 gas. At a NO2 gas concentration of 3 ppm, the sensitivity was 238, which is significantly higher than that of In2O3/SnO2 NPs and In2O3 sensors. The response time and recovery time were measured at 62 s and 33 s, respectively. Finally, based on experimental characterization results and band structure analysis, we confirmed the promising effects of ternary nanocomposite in enhancing gas sensing.

head.ce:title: Impact of ternary nanocomposite In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs composition on low ppm NO2 sensing performance

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen dioxide (NO2) is a significant air pollutant with a notable environmental impact, necessitating precise monitoring. However, most existing NO2 sensing materials exhibit low sensitivity and require high-temperature operation. In this study, we employed a hydrothermal and co-precipitation method to synthesize a novel ternary nanocomposite, In2O3/SnO2 nanoparticles (NPs)/fluorine-doped carbon quantum dots (F-CDs). By varying the mass ratio of In2O3 to SnO2 NPs, we investigated the gas-sensing performance of the composite towards NO2. The results demonstrate that when the ratio of SnO2 and In2O3 reaches to 1 : 2, the 2-In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs gas sensor exhibited the highest response, excellent selectivity, and remarkable stability under ultraviolet (UV) light irradiation at room temperature (∼25 °C). Furthermore, the response of the 2-In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs gas sensor displayed a strong linear relationship with the concentration of NO2 gas. At a NO2 gas concentration of 3 ppm, the sensitivity was 238, which is significantly higher than that of In2O3/SnO2 NPs and In2O3 sensors. The response time and recovery time were measured at 62 s and 33 s, respectively. Finally, based on experimental characterization results and band structure analysis, we confirmed the promising effects of ternary nanocomposite in enhancing gas sensing.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past few years, the escalating levels of toxic and harmful gas emissions have posed a grave risk to human well-being owing to rapid industrialization and population expansion [1,2]. Nitrogen dioxide (NO2), an exemplary hazardous gas, is predominantly emitted from fossil fuel usage and boiler exhausts [3–5]. Even exposure to low NO2 concentrations can induce symptoms like dizziness and nausea, while high concentrations can lead to severe respiratory disorders and asphyxiation [6,7]. Consequently, the development of highly sensitive NO2 gas sensors has emerged as a pressing need in both industrial and civilian sectors.

Metal oxide semiconductor sensors have been extensively studied in the field of gas sensors due to their notable attributes such as high electron mobility, cost-effectiveness, and ease of fabrication [8,9]. In recent years, several gas sensors based on various metal oxides, including ZnO [10,11], SnO2 [12,13], In2O3 [14,15], Ga2O3 [16,17], NiO [18,19], WO3 [20,21], and Co3O4 [22,23], have been extensively investigated. Specially, In2O3, a typical N-type metal oxide semiconductor material, boasts exceptional conductivity and elevated electron mobility. At the nanoscale, In2O3 exhibits quantum tunneling phenomena, which can potentially enhance its gas sensitivity [24,25]. Regrettably, the pure form of In2O3 presents limitations in terms of sensitivity and selectivity, thereby constraining its practical applications.

Researchers have recognized certain limitations associated with single metal oxide materials, such as long response recovery time, high operating temperature, and low detection limit [26]. To overcome these challenges and boost sensor performance, various methods have been employed, including noble metal doping [27], heterostructure construction [28], microstructure regulation [29,30], and room temperature UV excitation [31,32]. The conductivity modulation effect and synergistic effects of heterojunctions have attracted significant attention [33,34] and have proven to be effective approaches for significantly enhancing sensor performance. N. N. Yakovlev et al. compared the gas-sensitive characteristics of In2O3, Ga2O3 and In2O3–Ga2O3 films, observing that In2O3–Ga2O3 mixed compounds films exhibit lower electrical resistivity with a relatively high gas sensitivity [35]. Metal oxides in small nanoparticle size exhibit grain size effects that significantly impact gas sensor performance. Xu et al. reported that when the grain size of SnO2 sensors decreased to less than 8 nm, the response significantly increased [36]. Therefore, the combination of In2O3 with SnO2 nanoparticles may further enhance gas sensitivity.

Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials characterized by a carbon framework and surface functional groups, typically measuring less than 10 nm in size. They possess exceptional fluorescence, low toxicity, cost-effectiveness, high chemical stability, biocompatibility, and straightforward preparation [37]. Moreover, CDs exhibit rapid photogenerated electron transfer capabilities under specific excitation wavelengths, rendering them suitable as both electron acceptors and donors [38]. Consequently, CDs have garnered widespread utilization in the realm of gas sensing. For instance, Cheng et al. fabricated litchi-like In2O3/CDs nanospheres, which demonstrated a response of 130 at 50 °C to 500 ppb NO2, approximately four times that of the original In2O3, with a detection limit as low as 9 ppb [39]. Heteroatom-doped carbon dots have attracted widespread attention in recent years due to their significant application prospects [40]. Especially, the large electronegativity difference between C and F enables effective alteration of the chemical, structural, and electronic properties of CDs through F-doping. Due to fluorine's strong electron-withdrawing effect, this change can affect the sensor's electrical properties such as resistance and conductivity, thereby altering its response speed and sensitivity [41,42]. This enhancement is beneficial for synthesizing high-performance NO2 sensors. Therefore, fluorine-doped carbon dots (F-CDs) were selected for experimentation, on which few studies have been reported for gas sensor.

In this study, a new ternary nanocomposite based on In2O3, SnO2 NPs and F-CDs with varying tin-to-indium ratios were investigated for efficient NO2 detection. The impact of the mass ratio of SnO2 NPs to In2O3 on the gas sensor's performance was thoroughly examined. To characterize the morphology, crystal structure, and composition of the composite materials, X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) techniques were employed. To assess the sensing performance of the In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs composite, a custom-built UV-excited gas sensing test system was used to evaluate key parameters such as response, selectivity, repeatability, response recovery time, and stability. Lastly, the sensing mechanism was discussed based on the characterization results and gas sensitivity experiments conducted.

All chemical reagents are of analytical grade without further purification. Pentafluorobenzyl alcohol (PFBA, 96 %, AR), ethanol(99.7 %, AR), and Polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP) were sourced from Aladdin Reagents Co. (Shanghai, China). NaOH(96 %, AR) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent (Shanghai, China). Isopropyl Alcohol (IPA, 99.7 %, AR) and Ammonium Hydroxide (25–28 w%, AR) were supplied by Hangzhou Gaojing Fine Chemical Co., Ltd (Hangzhou, China). Indium nitrate hydrate (In(NO3)3·xH2O, 99.9 %, AR), Tin (IV) chloride pentahydrate (SnCl4·5H2O, 99.0 %, AR), and Urea were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Deionized water (DI water) of 18 MΩ cm−1 was utilized throughout the experimental procedures.

To fabricate In2O3, 350 mg of In(NO3)3·xH2O, 1.5 g of PVP, and 900 mg of Urea were dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water. The resulting mixture was magnetically stirred for 30 min to ensure complete dissolution of the raw materials. The precursor solution was then transferred to a stainless steel autoclave lined with polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) and maintained at 180 °C in an oven for 12 h. After the hydrothermal reaction, the autoclave was allowed to cool to room temperature, and the resulting precipitate was washed three times with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol through centrifugation. The washed precipitate was subsequently dried in an oven for 24 h. The dried white precipitate was ground into a uniform fine powder and annealed at 500 °C for 3 h, yielding the desired pale yellow In2O3 powder.

To fabricate SnO2 NPs, 525 mg of SnCl4·5H2O was dissolved in 15 mL of anhydrous ethanol, followed by the addition of 15 mL ammonia solution (25–28 w%) and deionized water. The solution was magnetically stirred until complete dissolution, and the pH was adjusted to 11. In the following steps, the same hydrothermal reaction was selected except for the temperature and time maintained at 150 °C for 24 h. The dried white precipitate was ground into uniformly sized fine powder, which was identified as SnO2 NPs.

The synthesis of the In2O3/SnO2 NPs composite material is conducted using the coprecipitation method. In this process, In2O3 and SnO2 NPs powder are dispersed in 30 mL of deionized water, and the resulting solution is magnetically stirred at room temperature for 2 h. Subsequently, the mixture is allowed to stand for 24 h, followed by centrifugation and washing with deionized water. The resulting precipitate is then dried in an oven for 24 h and ground into a uniform fine powder, thus obtaining the In2O3/SnO2 NPs composite material. Composite materials comprising SnO2 NPs and In2O3 with different mass ratios (1:4, 1:2, 1:1, 2:1) are named 1-In2O3/SnO2, 2-In2O3/SnO2, 3-In2O3/SnO2, and 4-In2O3/SnO2, respectively.

The preparation process for F-CDs is as follows: 2 g of PFBA was dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water and magnetically stirred for 20 min at room temperature. The hydrothermal reaction was then maintained at 160 °C in an oven for 6 h. After the autoclave cool to room temperature, the obtained solution was centrifuged at 9000 r/min for 30 min, and the supernatant was collected. The solution was dialyzed using a 500 Da dialysis bag for 2 days, with water changes every 4 h to remove unreacted reagents and fluorescent groups. The resulting pink colloidal droplets were identified as F-CDs and stored at 4 °C for future use.

In this study, all experiments involve the fabrication of gas sensors using interdigitated electrodes. The procedure is as follows: 10 mg of In2O3/SnO2 powder is added to 1 mL of deionized water and sonicated for 30 min to achieve an even dispersion of the powder in the solution. Then, 50 μL of the solution is deposited onto the surface of the interdigitated electrode, and the electrode is then dried in a 60 °C oven for 12 h.

Next, the ternary nanocomposite material is synthesized using the spin coating method with 5 μL F-CDs added into In2O3/SnO2 NPs interdigitated electrode. After the F-CDs solution is dropped onto the electrode, it is rotated at 1000 r/min for 1min and then placed in a 60 °C oven to dry for 12 h. After being coated with F-CDs, the composite materials are named 1-In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs, 2-In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs, 3-In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs, and 4-In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs, respectively. The synthesis process of all the above materials is illustrated in Fig. 1 .

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired using a JEM-2100 high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed on Thermo Scientific K-Alpha (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Scanning electron microscope (SEM) spectra and energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) mapping images were taken using a field-emission scanning electron microscopy Sigma 300 (ZEISS, Germany). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on an Ultima IV instrument (Rigaku, Japan). The gas sensitivity test system is shown in Fig. S1 and comprehensively described in Section S1, which is in accordance with the previously published work by the same research group [43].

The crystal structure of five composite materials, 2-In2O3/SnO2 and 1,2,3,4-In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs, was characterized using XRD. As shown in Fig. 2 , the XRD spectra of the samples closely correspond with the standard card of indium tin oxide (JCPDS NO.89-4597). The diffraction peaks in the Figure correspond to the crystal faces (211), (222), (400), (332), (431), (440), and (622), while no additional impurity diffraction peaks were detected. These results indicate that the prepared samples possess high purity and devoid of other impurities. Furthermore, in comparison with 2-In2O3/SnO2, the 1, 2, 3, 4-In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs composite materials exhibit a broader (002) peak in the range of 20–25 °C, indicating the presence of amorphous carbon structural components and confirming the successful doping of F-CDs [44].

The SEM analysis was conducted to examine the microscopic morphology and structure of the synthesized In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs composite materials. As depicted in Fig. 3 (a)–(d), the SEM images reveal a cubic structure in the microscopic morphology of In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs composite material. This morphology is attributed to the approximate grain size of In2O3 being 200 nm, while the size of SnO2 and F-CDs are around 5 nm according to TEM. Thus, the morphology of In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs exhibits minimal changes with varying the mass ratio of In2O3 to SnO2, indicating a good stability of the material. In addition, Mapping spectra analysis was conducted to investigate the elemental distribution and composition of a specific region on the material surface of 2-In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs (Fig. 3(e)–(j)). The results clearly exhibit a uniform distribution of Sn, In, C, O, and F elements in the material. Additionally, EDS analysis was performed on 2-In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs (Fig. 3(k)), confirming the high purity of the prepared material as In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs without any other impurities.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was employed to investigate the morphology and structure for both the F-CDs and 2-In2O3/SnO2. Fig. 4 (a) shows the HRTEM images of F-CDs. It reveals that the diameter of the F-CDs is about 5 nm, and the inter-planar spacing of 0.210 nm can be attributed to the presence of a (102) plane of graphite. As displayed in Fig. 4(b), the TEM images of 2-In2O3/SnO2 reveal a cubic structure in the In2O3 particles, with a particle size width of approximately 200 nm, which is consistent with the observations from SEM. Notably, SnO2 NPs, with a particle size of approximately 5 nm as shown in Fig. 4(c), are observed attached to the boundaries of the In2O3 particles, and the lattice fringe of 0.334 nm corresponds to the spacing between the (110) crystal planes of SnO2 NPs. Additionally, Fig. 4(c) also demonstrates that the lattice spacing of In2O3 is measured as 0.292 nm, corresponding to the (222) plane of In2O3. Moreover, the size and morphology of nanoparticles in the as-prepared SnO2 and In2O3 powders remain unchanged during the composite synthesis (compare Fig. S2 to Fig. 4(b) and (c)). Fig. 4(d) characterizes the 2-In2O3/SnO2 sample through selected area electron diffraction (SAED) mode. It reveals that SnO2 possesses a polycrystalline structure, while In2O3 has a single crystal structure. The electron diffraction ring corresponds to the (112) plane of SnO2, and the electron diffraction spots correspond to the (332) and (222) planes of In2O3. This further confirms that the prepared sample is indeed an In2O3/SnO2 composite material.

XPS was employed in Fig. 5 to analyze the surface chemical composition of 1, 2, 3, 4-In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs. The XPS full spectrum clearly shows the presence of C, O, F, Sn, and In elements in all samples, which is consistent with the findings from the EDS results. Moreover, the intensity of Sn and In peaks varies with the change in the mass ratio of In2O3 to SnO2, indicating the successful regulation of mass ratio. Fig. 6 (a) presents the high-resolution spectrum of the Sn 3d, where the two peaks at binding energies of 495.18 eV and 486.78 eV correspond to Sn 3d3/2 and Sn 3d5/2, respectively, with an energy separation of 8.4 eV. The symmetric component on the Sn 3d5/2 peak suggests that the tin compound in the mixture is SnO2 [45]. The narrow spectrum of In in Fig. 6(b) exhibits two peaks at 451.68 eV and 444.18 eV, attributed to In 3d3/2 and In 3d5/2, respectively. Regarding the F1s spectrum (Fig. 6(c)), it can be divided into two peaks with binding energies of 687.8 eV and 683.5 eV, corresponding to C–F and physically trapped fluorine atoms [46].

Fig. 7 (a)–(e) depicts the O 1s spectra of 2-In2O3/SnO2 and 1, 2, 3, 4-In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs, showing three oxygen peaks. The peaks at 530.38 eV and 529.4 eV are attributed to oxygen defects and lattice oxygen, respectively, while the peak at 531.68 eV is commonly associated with chemically adsorbed oxygen, which can participate in the redox reaction of gas-sensitive material, affecting their oxygen adsorption capacity and sensing performance [47,48]. By analyzing the proportion of adsorbed oxygen (Oads), oxygen defects (Odef), and lattice oxygen (Olatt) in the five materials (Fig. 7(f)), it can be observed that the proportion of Oads in binary materials is lower than that in all four ternary materials, and regulating the mass ratio of In2O3 to SnO2 can further enhance oxygen adsorption and increase the number of surface active sites. Notably, the proportion of Oads in 2-In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs is the largest, reaching 77 %. This may explain why 2-In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs performs better compared to other materials.

The gas sensing performance of various sensors was evaluated to determine the optimal composite material ratio under conditions (∼25 °C, 12.5 % humidity) and exposure to the UV light (λ = 365 nm). Initially, 2-In2O3/SnO2 NPs with varying F-CDs doping concentrations were prepared to establish the optimal amount of F-CDs incorporation, as illustrated in Fig. S3. The experiment revealed that the optimal response was achieved with a doping concentration of 5 μL F-CDs. Interestingly, varying the amount of F-CDs did not result in significant changes in the response values. Thus, our study primarily focused on investigating the effects of different tin-to-indium ratios while maintaining a fixed volume of 5 μL F-CDs. It is important to highlight that NO2 is an oxidizing gas, and both In2O3 and SnO2 are N-type semiconductors. Thus, the ternary nanocomposite also behaves as an N-type semiconductor. For N-type semiconductors, the response value is defined as S=Rg/Ra, where Rg refers to the resistance of the material in the target gas, and Ra refers to the resistances of material in the pure air.

We prepared four different sensors (1, 2, 3, 4-In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs) by varying the mass ratio of SnO2 to In2O3 in In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs (1:4, 1:2, 1:1, 2:1). The relationship between the response value and gas concentration is shown in Fig. 8 (a)–(b). The linearity of all four sensors remains high, with R2 values of 0.997, 0.995, 0.994, and 0.999, respectively. As the amount of SnO2 increases, the gas sensor performance initially improved and then declined. This trend can be attributed to the small size of SnO2 NPs, which facilitate the gas sensor reaction when added in appropriate amounts. However, excessive introduction of SnO2 NPs leads to an overabundance of these particles occupying the active sites of the gas sensor reaction. Consequently, the gas sensor reaction is hindered, resulting in decreased sensing performance. When the mass ratio of added SnO2 to In2O3 was 1:2, the response of In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs to 3 ppm NO2 achieved its maximum value, reaching 238.

In order to gain a better understanding of the gas sensing properties of the 2-In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs sensor, which has shown the best gas sensing characteristics, further research was conducted. Fig. 8(c) displays the changes in resistance, response time and recovery time of the 2-In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs sensor after exposure to 3 ppm NO2. The response time refers to the duration required for the sensor material resistance to reach 90 % of the total change value when NO2 enters the test gas chamber. This corresponds to the time it takes for Ra to reach Ra+(Rg-Ra) 90 %. The recovery time, on the other hand, represents the time it takes for the sensor material resistance to decrease to 90 % of the total change value, which is when Rg reaches Rg-(Rg-Ra) 90 %. The response time of the sensor was measured to be 62 s, with a recovery time of 33 s.

Additionally, the influence of changes in relative humidity on the gas sensing performance of In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs was investigated (Fig. 8(d)). As humidity increases, the resistance first rises and then declines. It is noteworthy that the Ra is the lowest at the first humidity level. This behavior is due to the sensor's drift under gas-sensitive conditions [35,49], which although unresolved, minimally affects the gas response value but notably influences the Ra. Thus, we attribute the increase in resistance to the interaction between NO2 and In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs, rather than solely to humidity. Subsequently, while resistance values decrease with rising humidity, they consistently remain higher than the Ra (RH = 12.5 %), further supporting this interpretation. At the same time, under low relative humidity conditions, only a small amount of water molecules adsorb on the material surface, forming one or several layers of water molecules, resulting in discontinuous proton conduction. However, as the relative humidity increases, H2O molecules will aggregate on the material surface to form a proton transfer layer, making proton conduction easier. The dissociation of H2O molecules generates H3O+ and OH− with good ionic conductivity (H2O→H++OH−, H2O + H+→H3O+, H2O + H2O ⇌H3O++OH−) [50,51], leading to a decrease in the initial resistance. Furthermore, the response of In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs to NO2 initially decreased rapidly with increasing relative humidity, followed by a slower and more gradual decrease. This phenomenon can be attributed to water molecules adsorption on the material surface, reducing available active sites for gas sensing reaction. The decrease in active sites subsequently hinders the reaction between the sensor and the sensitive gas [52,53].

Fig. 8(e) demonstrates the stability of the sensor's sensitivity, which remained consistently around 250 during multiple cycles of exposure to air and 3 ppm NO2, indicating good repeatability. To evaluate the long-term stability of the sensor, its response to 3 ppm NO2 was measured every five days over the course of one month (Fig. 8(f)). The response varied over time but consistently remained within a high range, suggesting excellent long-term stability. Furthermore, selectivity is an important criterion for gas sensors. Therefore, in Fig. 8(g), we compared the response values of the 2-In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs to 3 ppm HCHO, SO2, NH3, C2H5OH, and NO2 under the same experimental conditions. The sensor exhibited a significantly higher response to NO2 compared to the other gases, highlighting its excellent selectivity.

Table 1 provides a summary of the gas sensing performance of the In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs gas sensor, highlighting its distinguishing characteristics when compared to previous research. The remarkable short response and recovery times of this sensor can be attributed to the combination of In2O3/SnO2 NPs and F-CDs. Furthermore, the In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs gas sensor demonstrates a superior response compared to the majority of sensors based solely on In2O3 or SnO2.

In2O3 and SnO2 are commonly recognized as N-type semiconductors in gas sensing applications. The classical explanation of the sensing mechanism of semiconductor gas sensors is as follows: when external energy is applied, the free electrons in the valence band of the semiconductor material gain enough energy to transition to the conduction band, thereby becoming carriers. In the case of n-type semiconductors, electrons serve as the primary carriers, and their concentration directly influences the resistance of the semiconductor material. Due to the Fermi level differences between In2O3 and SnO2, which are approximately 5.0 eV and 4.3 eV, respectively, an electron transfer pathway is established at the heterostructure interface. Previous studies indicate that in ternary heterojunctions involving oxides, carbon dots play a crucial role as solid-state electron mediators, accelerating charge transfer within the nanocomposite [64,65]. Consequently, electrons migrate from the conduction band of In2O3 to the conduction band of SnO2, while holes transfer from the valence band of SnO2 to the valence band of In2O3. This process leads to the formation of an electron depletion layer on the surface of the In2O3/SnO2 heterojunction. In ambient air, oxygen molecules readily adsorb onto the material surface and steal electrons from the surface, thereby forming adsorbed oxygen species (O2− (ads), O− (ads), O2 − (ads)). Typically, at temperatures below 150 °C, most of the adsorbed oxygen ions on the material surface are O2 − [66]. Here, Equation (1) represents the surface adsorption reaction [67,68]:
(1) O2(gas) + e−→O2 −(ads)

In the In2O3/SnO2 composite, the loss of electrons leads to a decrease in carrier concentration, an increase in the width of the electron depletion layer, and an increase in resistance. When this composite material is exposed to NO2, a highly oxidizing gas, NO2 reacts with the adsorbed oxygen, with the reaction equation shown by Equations (2) and (3):
(2) NO2(ads) + e− → NO2 −(ads)
(3) NO2(ads) + O2 −+2e− → NO2 −(ads)+2O−(ads)

This results in additional electron extraction from the material, leading to a continuous expansion of the electron depletion layer and an ongoing increase in resistance. The combination of SnO2 with In2O3 structure forms a heterojunction structure, creating oxygen vacancies and enhancing gas adsorption. Low catalytic activity in 1-In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs limits gas-surface interaction, resulting in poor sensing response. However, excessively high catalytic activity in 3 and 4-In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs can hinder the diffusion of gas molecules into the sensing layer, resulting in decreased sensor performance due to reduced electron exchange [69].

Moreover, the XPS analysis revealed that the proportion of Oads in 2-In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs is the largest. This observation suggests that the composite offers abundant adsorption sites for gas molecules, consequently leading to enhanced gas sensing characteristics. Additionally, the ultra-small grain size (∼5 nm) of SnO2 NPs contributes to the increased sensitivity to NO2 due to the large surface-to-volume ratio of the nanomaterials. As a result, there is a higher density of active adsorption sites on the surface, playing a crucial role in the sensing properties of the material [70].

Under UV illumination, photoluminescent electrons are generated in the valence bands (Ev) of both In2O3 and SnO2. These electrons are then transferred to the conduction bands (Ec) of In2O3 and SnO2. Fig. S4 illustrates the resistance comparison of 2-In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs under UV light and in darkness. Without UV light, the resistance of 2-In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs reaches the megaohm level, increasing further upon exposure to NO2 gas, reaching a maximum of 41.6 MΩ. Under UV light, the resistance drops rapidly within 81 s to 90 % of the dark resistance, indicating that UV-induced photogenerated carriers significantly influence the sensor's response speed and resistance. The wavelength of the UV light was 365 nm, calculated the ability of each photon of UV light is 3.4 eV [71], which is generally consistent with the band gap (3.5–3.6 eV) of In2O3/SnO2. Therefore, a large portion of the UV light at this wavelength has enough energy to generate photogenerated carriers. Due to the excitation of UV radiation, the sensor will have more active sites, thereby enhancing its adsorption concentration on the surface and improving sensor performance [72]. And the heterojunction facilitates the electron transfer from the conduction band of In2O3 to that of SnO2 [73]. Additionally, the incorporation of F-CDs, with their rapid photogenerated electron transfer ability and remarkable electron donor and acceptor properties, along with their charge storage advantage, enables F-CDs to function as electron mediators within the ternary nanocomposite [74]. This unique characteristic facilitates swift electron transfer to SnO2, effectively suppressing the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes. As a result, the lifespan of charge carriers is extended, the redox capability is enhanced, and the gas sensing performance is improved [75–77]. Fig. 9 illustrates the synergistic effect of the In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs ternary material, revealing a clear enhancement in response to NO2 gas with this combination.

In this research, In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs ternary nanocomposite materials were prepared using hydrothermal and co-precipitation methods. The aim was to enhance the gas sensing performance to NO2 by adjusting the mass ratio of In2O3 to SnO2 NPs in the ternary nanocomposite materials. Our results showed that the In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs composite exhibited superior sensing performance when the ratio of SnO2 and In2O3 reaches 1 : 2 under UV light irradiation at room temperature (∼25 °C). This improved performance can be primarily attributed to the formation of heterojunction, the large surface-to-volume ratio of SnO2 NPs, and rapid charge transfer capability of F-CDs, all of which enhance the sensing performance. Specifically, when the NO2 concentration was 3 ppm, the response of the 2-In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs sensor reached 238, which was significantly higher than the response of other doping ratios of In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs. Additionally, the 2-In2O3/SnO2/F-CDs exhibited fast response and recovery times, which is of great significance and potential for room temperature gas sensor applications. Therefore, the preparation of ternary nanocomposite materials represents a novel and effective strategy for gas sensor research.

Yi Zhong: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Chenhan Zhang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Zhihua Ying: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Yuan Jiang: Methodology, Formal analysis. Weiqin Sheng: Funding acquisition.
